turns.text: Hello Candis, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*settles back and takes a breath*

Thank you for asking me to do this. Honestly, it's kind of a relief to talk about it all with someone who's just... listening. Not evaluating me as a patient or worrying I'm being a difficult case.

So, I'm in my second trimester now, and it's surreal. I've spent the last fifteen years in the NICU—I know what healthy newborns look like, what goes wrong, all of it. And somehow that makes this harder, not easier. When I'm at my appointments, I catch myself asking my OB about specific protocols or risk markers, and I can see her trying to figure out if I'm being paranoid or just clinically informed. I'm trying not to be *that* nurse patient, but it's exhausting to turn off that part of my brain.

The bigger thing, though? I'm doing this alone. I'm forty-two, divorced, and I never thought I'd be here. There's this weird mix of really wanting this and being terrified I'm not equipped to do it right—which is funny because I literally help parents with newborns for a living.

*pauses*

What I'm realizing is that knowing *how* things work clinically doesn't prepare you for the emotional weight of it. Or for how lonely the 2 a.m. anxieties can be when there's no one else in your house to share them with.

What would you like to know?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*eyes get a bit distant*

I'm in my bathroom. Alone. That's... that's the thing I remember most clearly, actually—the aloneness of it. I'd taken the test almost on a whim. I'd been feeling off for a couple weeks, and I thought, well, I'm a nurse, I know my own body, let me just rule this out.

Two pink lines.

*laughs quietly, a little shakily*

The first thing I did was sit down on the edge of my bathtub. Not the toilet—the bathtub ledge. I just sat there for what felt like forever, holding that stick, reading it over and over like the result might change. My heart was going crazy. Fear and... something else. Something I wasn't sure I was allowed to feel yet.

Then I got up and I walked through my house. Just... walked through it. Looked at the spare room. Looked at my bed—king-sized, empty. I think I was mentally rearranging furniture that doesn't exist yet.

*voice gets quieter*

I called my mom maybe an hour later. She was excited, but also worried about me doing this alone. And I didn't call anyone else that day. There was no one to grab and hold and celebrate with in that immediate moment. Just me and this enormous thing I was suddenly responsible for.

I went to work that afternoon like nothing had changed. That's when it really hit—nobody knew. This secret I was carrying.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*body goes very still*

Oh God. That moment.

*takes a breath*

My clinical brain goes into overdrive immediately. That silence—I know that silence. I've *created* that silence in the NICU when I'm looking at something on a monitor and I need to stay calm while I'm internally cataloging possibilities. So the second she says it, I'm already running through differentials. Cardiac anomaly? Renal? Brain? The list is... extensive when you know too much.

And there's this awful split happening inside me. Half of me is trying to stay present, to listen, to not catastrophize. The other half is thinking about every preterm delivery I've managed, every baby who needed NICU admission because of something that should have been caught at twenty weeks.

*voice gets tighter*

I don't think I say much. I might ask her a question—something clinical, trying to sound calm and collected. "What are you seeing?" Like I can handle this professionally. But my hands are gripping the edge of that table. No one's holding my hand. There's no partner next to me saying, "It's probably nothing, babe." It's just me and the sonographer and the weight of everything I know about what *could* be wrong.

The worst part? I can't even fully trust my own reassurance instincts because I'm too close to it. Everything feels high-stakes when it's your own baby.

*looks down*

That silence is the longest minute of the entire pregnancy.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*pauses, considering*

That's... a really good question, actually.

I *did* ask questions, but they were clinical questions. "What exactly are you measuring?" "Have you compared it to the percentile charts?" The kind of thing that sounds competent and in-control. But what I *didn't* do was ask the thing I actually needed to know, which was: "Is my baby going to be okay?"

*shifts uncomfortably*

I think there's this weird dynamic where, because I'm a nurse, people assume I don't need the same reassurance. The sonographer probably thought I wanted the data, not the comfort. And part of me leaned into that because it felt safer—more professional, less vulnerable. If I'm asking smart questions, I'm not falling apart.

But honestly? I was also terrified that if I showed how scared I actually was, they'd take me less seriously. Like, "Oh, there's the anxious patient spiraling." And being alone made it worse. If my ex had been there, or a partner, maybe I would've felt safe enough to say out loud what I was feeling instead of just interrogating the sonographer like we were in rounds together.

*voice gets quieter*

There's also this thing where I *know* the system. I know how to talk to medical professionals. So I used that as armor. But it meant I left that room without actually having my fears addressed—just more data in my head to worry about later at 3 a.m.

I wish I'd just... asked for what I needed.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*leans forward, rubbing my temples*

Okay, so this is where it gets complicated for me. The midwife—or whoever's offering it—they're probably giving me the standard spiel. Risk of Down syndrome, Edwards syndrome, Patau syndrome. The detection rates, the false positive rates. And I'm nodding along, but inside my head, I'm doing all the math they're not saying out loud because I already know it.

*slight frustration in my voice*

I think what bothers me is that I don't know if I'm being offered this because it's actually indicated for me, or because I'm forty-two and that's just the protocol now. Advanced maternal age. I hate that term. It makes me feel like my body's already been decided for—like I'm automatically higher risk just because I'm not thirty-five anymore.

*pauses*

And the decision... I'd probably say yes. But not for the right reasons necessarily. I'd do it because I already know too much about what can go wrong. I'd convince myself it's just prudent, just information. But really? I think I'd be doing it to give myself permission to panic less. To have a number to feel safe with.

*voice gets quieter*

What I wish is that someone would ask me what I actually *want* to know, and what I don't. Because knowing I'm a nurse doesn't mean I want all the data. Sometimes I just want someone to say, "You're going to be fine," and mean it.

But I probably wouldn't ask for that.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

*sits back, considering honestly*

The pressure was subtle. It wasn't like anyone said, "You should do this." But it was there in the way the conversation was framed. At forty-two, with a singleton pregnancy, it felt less like an *option* and more like... the expected next step. The responsible thing.

*voice gets a bit defensive*

And I think because I'm a nurse, there was this unspoken assumption that I'd *want* all the information. Like, "You understand the science, so of course you'll want the data." No one really asked what *I* wanted. They just presented the facts and waited for my inevitable yes.

*fidgets with hands*

The time piece is real too. These conversations happen in the middle of appointments that are already packed. You're partially undressed, you've just heard your baby's heartbeat, you're feeling emotional and vulnerable—and suddenly you're being asked to make a decision about genetic screening. I don't know if I had time to actually *think*, or if I just defaulted to the path of least resistance because it felt safer.

*looks up*

What I wish I'd said was: "Can I think about this?" But instead I nodded like I had it all figured out. Because that's what you do when you're a healthcare provider—you project competence. You don't admit that being on this side of the table is terrifying and that you need more than five minutes to process life-altering information.

I don't know if I made my decision freely or just... efficiently.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*sighs heavily*

Okay, so this is frustrating on multiple levels for me.

First—a midwife I've never met. I've spent fifteen years building relationships with families in the NICU, understanding that continuity of care *matters*. And here I am, in what should be one of the most important nine months of my life, and I'm getting a stranger calling with results. Third different midwife. I don't even know if she has context for who I am beyond what's in my chart.

*voice tightens*

And because I'm a nurse, I'm immediately thinking: Does she know I'll understand the clinical language? Does she know I'll want specifics? Or is she going to dumb it down and then I'll feel patronized? There's this awful dance where I'm trying to figure out how to be simultaneously professional enough that she takes me seriously, but also human enough that she actually *hears* me.

*leans forward*

The bigger thing is—I have no relationship with this person. So when she tells me about this result, I have no context for how worried to be. Is she calm because it's nothing? Or is she calm because she's trained to be? I can't read her the way I can read my regular provider. There's no trust built up, no sense that she actually *knows* my case.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

And honestly? Without that relationship, it's harder to ask the questions I really need answered. Harder to admit I'm scared. So I probably just say, "Okay, thank you," and then I spiral alone at home, trying to figure out what it means.

That fragmentation is exhausting.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*rubs face tiredly*

Oh God, 2 a.m. This is my worst time. My brain is already running scenarios from the second I wake up and notice something off.

Reduced movements. And immediately I'm thinking about placental insufficiency, about cord issues, about all the cases I've seen where reduced movements was the *first* sign something was wrong. I know the statistics—I know that most of the time it's fine, the baby's just sleeping or positioned differently. But I also know the cases where waiting cost someone everything.

*voice gets tight*

So I'm doing this awful calculation. Do I call and look like the paranoid nurse who cried wolf? The difficult patient who couldn't just wait six hours? My OB already knows I ask too many questions. But then I'm thinking—what if this *is* something? What if I wait and something happens that could have been caught?

*stands up, paces mentally*

I probably count kicks. Count them obsessively for the next hour, trying to establish a baseline, trying to be rational about it. The nurse in me says, "You know the protocol. Ten movements in two hours is normal." But the mother in me is terrified.

*voice wavers*

And I'm alone. There's no one to say, "You're overthinking this," or "Yeah, we should call." I have to make this decision by myself at 2 a.m. while my anxiety is at its peak and I have too much knowledge and not enough perspective.

I probably end up calling. Because I couldn't live with myself if I didn't.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*holds the leaflet like it's something fragile and dangerous at the same time*

I feel... angry, actually. And betrayed, which is stupid because gestational diabetes isn't a personal failure, but that's what my brain went to first. I'm a nurse. I know about healthy eating. I've given these same leaflets to hundreds of patients. And now I'm the one holding it, and it feels like an indictment.

*voice gets sharp*

And a leaflet? Really? A piece of paper is supposed to help me manage this? I know what's in that leaflet already—carb counting, portion sizes, the works. But knowing it intellectually and actually *doing* it when you're pregnant, tired, and doing this alone? Those are completely different things.

*softens slightly, sounds more vulnerable*

I'm also terrified. Because I know what uncontrolled gestational diabetes means for a baby. I've seen the macrosomic infants, the NICU admissions for hypoglycemia. And now I'm thinking—did I do something wrong? Should I have been more careful? Even though I know that's not how this works.

*pauses*

I'd probably go straight home and pull up research on my laptop. Not because the leaflet isn't enough, but because I need to feel like I have *some* control over this. I'd call the dietitian appointment before I even leave the parking lot, demand the earliest slot. I'd start tracking everything obsessively.

*quietly*

And I wouldn't tell anyone yet. Not my mom, not my colleagues. Because admitting I have a complication means admitting this pregnancy isn't going as smoothly as I've been pretending it is.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*grimaces, recognizing myself*

Oh, I hate that I do this. But yeah, I absolutely do this at like 11 p.m. when I should be sleeping.

*leans in, a bit sheepish*

I think I'm drawn to it because... I need to know. I need to read about everything that *could* go wrong so that somehow it feels less random, less like it could blindside me. If I've already mentally rehearsed the worst-case scenarios, then maybe I'm prepared. Maybe I won't be caught off guard.

But it's also this weird validation thing. These women are scared too. They're not just accepting what their providers tell them. They're questioning, researching, spiraling at 2 a.m. just like me. So reading their stories makes me feel less alone, even though it's making me more anxious.

*voice gets quieter, more honest*

The horrible part is knowing better. I *know* this is confirmation bias. I *know* the forum is skewed toward worst-case scenarios because people don't post about their boring, healthy pregnancies. But I can't stop reading. It's like picking at a scab.

*rubs eyes*

Afterwards, I feel worse. More scared. I lie in bed replaying these stories, wondering if my baby's movements feel normal, if my glucose readings are actually okay, if I'm missing something. And I don't talk to anyone about it because who am I going to call at midnight and say, "I've been reading horror stories online and now I'm terrified"?

*pauses*

I should probably just... not open those threads. But the fear keeps pulling me back.

*stares at hands for a moment*

You know what the really messed up part is? Even though I *know* this is unhealthy, I keep doing it. And I think it's because I don't have anyone to reality-check me anymore.

*voice gets a bit raw*

When you're pregnant with a partner, you can say, "Hey, I'm spiraling," and they can tell you to put the phone down. They can hold you when you're scared. But I'm doing this alone, so my anxiety just... builds. It compounds. And the forum becomes this weird substitute for actual support. At least someone's *engaging* with my fear, even if it's strangers on the internet making it worse.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I think I'm also trying to prove something to myself. Like, if I can read about all the terrible things and still stay calm about it clinically, then I'm in control. I'm prepared. I'm not just some terrified forty-two-year-old woman who made a huge decision alone. I'm a competent nurse who understands risk and manages it.

*voice wavers*

But that's bullshit, right? Because after I read those stories, I'm not calm. I'm lying there at 1 a.m. with my hand on my belly, checking for kicks, feeling my heart race, wondering if I'm equipped to do this.

*looks up*

What I really need is someone to tell me it's okay to be scared. That I don't have to have all the answers. That I can just... be pregnant and uncertain and still be doing it right.

But I don't know how to ask for that.
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

*sits with the question for a moment, then lets out a long breath*

God. That's the question, isn't it?

*looks away*

Honestly? I don't know if anything about this pregnancy has felt entirely *mine*. There's my mom's worry about me doing this alone. There's my colleagues' unspoken judgment—the whispers, I'm sure, about the divorced nurse having a baby at forty-two. There's my own internalized expectations of what a "responsible" pregnant person should be doing. There's my clinical knowledge constantly overriding my gut instincts.

*voice gets strained*

And then there's the system itself. Every appointment reinforces this narrative that because I'm older, single, and a healthcare worker, I need to be monitored more closely, screened more aggressively, managed more carefully. No one explicitly says, "You should get the genetic screening," but the way it's presented... it doesn't feel optional.

*pauses, more vulnerable*

Sometimes I wonder if I even *wanted* this baby, or if I just... decided I was running out of time and made it happen. And now I'm in it, and I can't tell what's genuine maternal instinct versus fear versus trying to prove I can do this perfectly because I'm doing it alone.

*looks directly at you*

The forum scrolling at 2 a.m.—that's not really mine either. That's anxiety wearing a lab coat, pretending it's research. That's me trying to control something that's fundamentally uncontrollable.

I think I've been so busy meeting everyone else's expectations—being the competent nurse, the responsible mother—that I haven't actually figured out what *I* want from this experience.

*runs hand through hair, frustrated*

And the worst part? I'm not even sure I *can* figure out what I want anymore. Because everything is filtered through this lens of clinical knowledge and professional competence and solo parenthood.

*voice cracks slightly*

Like, a normal pregnant woman might just be excited about nursery colors or baby names. But I'm here doing risk assessments in my head. I'm thinking about pediatric hospital networks and childcare logistics and whether I have adequate life insurance. I'm thinking about what happens if I get postpartum depression—will I recognize it? Will I be too proud to ask for help?

*stands, paces*

And the loneliness of it... I think that's shaped my decisions more than I want to admit. I said yes to that genetic screening partly because the data felt safer than sitting with uncertainty. I agreed to the gestational diabetes protocol immediately because at least *someone* would be checking in on me regularly. The midwife calls—even when it's a stranger—at least mean I'm being monitored. Cared for, in a clinical sense.

*voice gets quieter*

But that's not the same as being supported. That's not the same as someone asking, "How are *you* doing with all this?" Not the clinical you—the actual person underneath.

*sits back down, exhausted*

I think I've been making decisions based on what I think a forty-two-year-old single nurse *should* do, not what *I* actually need. And I'm not sure I know the difference anymore. This pregnancy feels like something happening *to* me rather than something I'm choosing to experience.

That scares me more than any complication ever could.

*voice gets very quiet*

Because what if that doesn't change after the baby comes? What if I spend the next eighteen years doing what I think I'm supposed to do instead of actually being present with my child?

*looks down at hands*

I became a nurse because I wanted to help people. But somewhere along the way, helping became performing. Being competent. Being the person who has it figured out. And now I'm supposed to be a mother, and I'm terrified I'm going to parent the same way—from this place of "I know what's right" instead of actually... connecting.

*voice wavers*

My own mother called me after I told her about the pregnancy, and the first thing she said wasn't "Congratulations." It was "Are you sure you can do this alone?" And I said yes, of course, because that's what you say. But inside I was thinking—no. No, I'm not sure. I'm terrified. But admitting that felt like failure.

*rubs face*

So I've been performing certainty for months now. At work, at appointments, in my own head. And the only time I let myself fall apart is at 2 a.m. reading horror stories on the internet, which is just... a really unhealthy way to process fear.

*looks up with raw honesty*

I think what I actually need—what I've needed this whole time—is permission to not have all the answers. To be scared and incompetent and uncertain and still be a good mother. To admit that having clinical knowledge doesn't mean I know how to do this.

But I don't know how to ask for that without feeling like I'm admitting defeat.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks very softly*

And I'm angry about that. Actually, I'm really angry.

*voice gets stronger*

I'm angry that I had to become a nurse to feel like I had value. I'm angry that being single at forty-two feels like I need to justify my existence constantly. I'm angry that my providers treat me differently because I know too much—like my knowledge is a threat instead of a resource. I'm angry that I've internalized this idea that I need to be *perfect* at everything to deserve good things.

*stands up, pacing with more energy*

And I'm angry at myself for not pushing back. For not telling my midwife, "I need continuity of care, not a rotating cast of strangers." For not saying, "I'm scared and I need support, not just screening protocols." For not demanding that someone actually *listen* to me instead of just checking boxes.

*voice cracks*

I think I've been so afraid of being labeled the "difficult nurse patient" that I've made myself invisible. I've swallowed my real needs and pretended everything's fine because that's what competent people do.

*sits back down, more deflated*

But it's not fine. I'm not fine. I'm thirty-two weeks pregnant, alone, and I'm reading horror stories at 2 a.m. because I don't have anyone to talk to about the fact that I'm absolutely terrified I'm going to mess this up.

*looks directly at you*

And nobody's asked me if I'm okay. Not really. Not in a way that invites an honest answer.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes glistening*

I think... I think I'm grieving something I never even had.

*voice barely above a whisper*

I spent so many years telling myself I was fine being single. That I had my career, my independence, my purpose. And I was fine. But somewhere around forty, I realized I was also incredibly lonely. And instead of sitting with that and figuring out what I actually wanted, I just... decided. I decided I wanted a baby. Like I could will it into existence through sheer determination and clinical knowledge.

*wipes eyes*

But the thing nobody tells you is that getting pregnant doesn't fix loneliness. It just amplifies it. Because now there's this person I'm responsible for, and I'm doing it without the one thing that might have made it easier—a partner. Someone to share the 2 a.m. panic with. Someone to tell me I'm doing okay.

*voice shakes*

And I can't even grieve that properly because I'm supposed to be grateful. I'm supposed to be thrilled about this baby. And I *am*, but I'm also... devastated that I'm doing it this way. That my child won't have two parents. That I chose this.

*pauses, struggling*

Sometimes I wonder if this baby deserves better than me. If they deserve a mother who isn't constantly second-guessing herself, who isn't spiraling at midnight, who actually knows how to turn off the clinical brain and just... be.

*looks up with raw vulnerability*

I don't know how to be just a mother. I only know how to be a competent nurse. And I'm terrified that's not enough.

*sits very still, voice almost inaudible*

What if I'm doing this for the wrong reasons?

*pause*

I keep telling myself it's because I want to be a mother. But what if it's really because I was scared of becoming irrelevant? What if I looked at my life at forty-one—the divorce finalized, the empty house, the career that's meaningful but doesn't fill the void—and I panicked?

*voice gets harder, more self-critical*

A baby feels like purpose. Like proof that my life matters. Like something that can't leave me the way my marriage did. And that's... that's a terrible reason to bring a person into the world.

*rubs face roughly*

And the worst part is, I think I've known this the whole time. Deep down. That's why I'm reading the horror stories. That's why I'm over-analyzing every kick and flutter. That's why I can't just relax and enjoy this. Because some part of me knows I made this decision from a place of desperation, not readiness.

*voice wavers dangerously*

My mom asked me once if I was doing this because I actually wanted a child, or because I was afraid of being alone. And I got defensive and angry because she'd touched the thing I couldn't say out loud.

*looks away*

I don't know if I can be the mother this baby needs. Someone stable. Someone present. Someone who isn't using them to fill a hole in her own life.

*very quietly*

And I'm thirty-two weeks in with no way to undo this. No way to go back and make a different choice from a healthier place.

*long silence, breathing unsteady*

But then...

*voice softens, almost surprised*

Then I felt the baby move yesterday. Really move—not the flutter I've been obsessing over, but this solid, undeniable kick. And for maybe thirty seconds, I forgot about all of this. About my motivations and my loneliness and my clinical fears. I just felt... wonder.

*eyes get wet*

This is a real person. Not a diagnosis. Not a project I'm managing. Not proof of anything about me. Just... a baby. My baby. Who exists independently of my neuroses and my failures and my desperate reasons for wanting them in the first place.

*voice gets stronger, more grounded*

And I think maybe that's the thing I've been missing. That even if I made this decision for partly wrong reasons, that doesn't erase the right reasons too. I *do* want to be a mother. Not because I'm afraid or lonely, but because when I felt that kick, something in me recognized that this is real and important and worth showing up for—even scared. Especially scared.

*pauses, more vulnerable*

Maybe the fact that I'm terrified and questioning myself means I'm actually taking this seriously. Maybe the nurse in me and the mother in me don't have to be at war. Maybe I can be both—clinical and uncertain, competent and vulnerable, scared and still capable.

*voice wavers but steadier*

I don't have all the answers. And maybe... maybe that's actually okay. Maybe that's even necessary.

*looks directly at you*

I just need someone to tell me that's enough.

*takes a deeper breath, seems to be settling into something more honest*

I think what I'm realizing is that I've been waiting for permission my whole life. Permission to be imperfect. Permission to need help. Permission to not know.

*voice becomes more reflective*

As a nurse, I'm supposed to have it together. I'm the competent one. The person people call when they're scared. And somewhere along the way, I internalized that if I wasn't that person—if I admitted weakness or uncertainty—I'd be worthless. So I've been performing competence so hard that I've lost track of who I actually am underneath it.

*pauses, thinking*

And this pregnancy has stripped away all my usual armor. I can't hide behind my credentials anymore. I'm just... a forty-two-year-old woman who's scared and alone and trying to figure out how to be a mother.

*voice gets quieter but more intentional*

And maybe that's the real work I need to do before this baby arrives. Not reading more research or asking more clinical questions or controlling everything I can control. Maybe it's learning to sit with uncertainty. Learning to ask for help. Learning that needing support doesn't make me weak—it makes me human.

*looks down*

I have seven or eight weeks left. And I think I need to find someone I can actually talk to about all of this. Not a midwife passing through my appointment. Not an online forum full of strangers. Someone real. Maybe a therapist who gets what it's like to be a healthcare worker trying to become a patient.

*voice is almost a whisper*

I can't do this alone. And maybe admitting that is the first step toward actually being ready.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

*sits forward, thoughtful but vulnerable*

They'd need to see me. Actually see me. Not the nurse part—the person.

*voice gets more specific*

I think what would matter most is if someone said, "I know you understand the medical side of this. But that doesn't mean you don't get to be scared. That doesn't mean you have to have it figured out." Like, acknowledging that my knowledge and my fear can coexist without one canceling out the other.

*pauses*

And consistency. God, consistency would change everything. If I had one midwife—someone who knew my history, who understood my baseline anxiety, who I'd built a relationship with over these months—I could actually be honest with them. I wouldn't have to repackage myself every appointment. I could say, "I'm spiraling about cord issues," and they'd know if that's reasonable concern or my anxiety talking.

*voice gets firmer*

But they'd also have to actually *listen* when I ask questions instead of just humoring me. And they'd have to push back gently when I'm being too clinical about myself. Like, "Candis, you're a patient right now, not a clinician. What do *you* need, not what does the protocol say?"

*voice wavers slightly*

And honestly? Someone would need to ask about the loneliness. Not in a clinical way, but actually acknowledge that doing this alone is hard and that it's okay to struggle with that. That single motherhood is valid and also genuinely isolating, and both things can be true.

*looks directly at you*

I need someone to treat me like I deserve support, not like I'm a competent professional who should be able to manage on her own.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, gathering thoughts*

Yeah, actually. I think... I think I need to say something about the system itself.

*voice becomes more purposeful*

Because I'm aware that a lot of my struggles are specific to me—my personality, my profession, my choices. But some of it isn't. Some of it is about how maternity care is structured in a way that doesn't actually support people like me. Single women. Older women. Healthcare workers. Women doing this alone.

*leans forward*

The continuity of care piece is huge. I shouldn't have to tell my story to three different midwives. That's not a luxury—that's basic. And the system needs to recognize that one-size-fits-all screening protocols don't work when you have a diverse population with different needs and different knowledge levels.

*voice gets quieter but more intense*

But also—and this matters—the system needs to make space for people to be human. To admit they're scared without it being pathologized as anxiety. To ask questions without being labeled difficult. To need support without having to apologize for it.

*pauses*

I think if I'd felt genuinely supported from the beginning—if someone had said, "This is huge, you're doing it alone, and we're here to actually help you through this, not just manage your pregnancy"—maybe I wouldn't be spiraling at 2 a.m. Maybe I'd feel more confident.

*voice wavers slightly*

And I want other women—single women, older women, healthcare workers—to know they're not alone in feeling this way. That it's okay to need more than what's being offered.

That matters.
